Perhaps the most challenging thing about standing on principle as an elected official is that when you take a public position on a controversial item, people frequently judge you not on the basis of your reasoning applied to facts but instead on the basis of whatever narrative has taken hold.
I don't really blame most people.
It's hard for someone at home to know precisely what's going on in Lansing or Washington or anywhere else for that matter. We're all busy with our own lives. We rely on individuals and organizations we trust--often politicians or media, as much as we hate to admit it--to distill complex public policy matters or legislation into something more accessible.
Of course, the politicians and media we rely on routinely take shortcuts themselves. They're also busy and have deadlines to meet. They rarely read bills or dig deep into subjects they're covering, so they too rely on others for summaries and talking points.
Given how this all unfolds, especially when partisanship and bias get tossed into the mix, it's hardly surprising that misinformation abounds. When a narrative becomes widespread, it's easy to get sucked into thinking that an alternative view is wrong simply because it's not one believed by most people. If 433 members of Congress say one thing, and Justin Amash and Thomas Massie say another, it's difficult to accept that the lone dissenters might be right.
In fact, the main reason I developed the practice of using Facebook to explain all my votes in the Michigan Legislature and Congress was that I knew I couldn't depend on my colleagues or the press to accurately present information to the public. It wasn't just about making sure my constituents knew how I was voting; it was also about making sure we were all working with the same set of facts.
It's in that spirit that I bring you this piece titled "The debt ceiling isn't what you think it is."
[Upgrade to paid](https://substack..x2EIEYD6xbqHa0qFR-6dCA0GgnfC8Gs9k3z05hIPWZo?&utm_medium=email&utm_source=subscribe-widget&utm_content=120824711)
As you may be aware, Treasury Secretary Janet Yellen has been [signaling](https://substack.com/redirect/0b86ce49-a642-4912-9797-9b32c38b78b4?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) that if Congress fails to raise the debt ceiling by as early as June 1, the United States won't be able to "pay its bills." She's even gone so far as to [warn](https://substack.com/redirect/bb958acf-bd9c-4e88-a332-030f9af3da9a?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) that "we face economic and financial catastrophe" if Congress doesn't act.
The secretary isn't the only one predicting doom if the stalemate between President Joe Biden and congressional Republicans isn't resolved. You can't watch cable news or scroll through your Twitter feed without seeing scores of pundits imploring politicians on Capitol Hill to act immediately to avert the impending crisis.
We repeat this drama every year or two, with the president and administration officials proclaiming that the debt ceiling must be hiked or suspended unconditionally and the opposition party in Congress demanding concessions in exchange for their votes to increase the government's borrowing authority.
The prevailing position of each party reverses depending on who's in the White House and who controls Congress. Partisans try hard to memory-hole these contradictory performances from leading figures in their party, but they're pretty easy to pull up.
| [](https://substack.com/redirect/1fd2edeb-6321-4af7-8eff-5eb4b37f45e2?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)|
---|---|---
| [](https://substack.com/redirect/c4a7c8f6-9977-4af1-b374-041b11eb86e1?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)|
---|---|---
What's obvious is that the people we elect don't take the debt ceiling seriously. If they did, then they wouldn't be swapping positions so readily. But it makes perfect sense that they don't take it seriously. They don't take the _debt_ seriously.
With rare exception, Democrats and Republicans in Washington aren't concerned about the long-term ramifications of endless borrowing: inflation, high interest rates, economic stagnation. They're looking no further than the next election; they operate in the here and now. What's clear to all of them is that, at the end of any battle over the debt ceiling, a modest deal will be worked out to approve more borrowing, because both parties are addicted to spending. They need that chedda!
In the meantime, they're putting on a show for their respective bases, with each side performing its role in this year's production. Regardless of which part they've been assigned this season, Democratic and Republican leaders will make the same insincere assertion to voters: _We 're the responsible ones_.
It's a theater of the absurd, and it's bolstered by countless talking heads on television and social media spreading spurious claims about the debt ceiling. Let's take a look at the most common myths surrounding this perennial spectacle.
[Share](https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=1137026&post_id=120824711&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email&utm_content=share&action=share&triggerShare=true&isFreemail=true&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjo5MDUwMTE5MiwicG9zdF9pZCI6MTIwODI0NzExLCJpYXQiOjE2ODQ4Njg1MTEsImV4cCI6MTY4NzQ2MDUxMSwiaXNzIjoicHViLTExMzcwMjYiLCJzdWIiOiJwb3N0LXJlYWN0aW9uIn0.5ycRXbX8vzeYgxfP48A_ms9B1hKSrxAGhCRDHmU3BMU)
### Myth #1: If Congress repeals the debt ceiling, then the Treasury can simply borrow whatever amount the government needs
One of the most common things I'll read or hear whenever the debt ceiling is in the news is that Congress should just repeal the debt ceiling; when the House and Senate vote to approve a budget, they're implicitly voting to approve any necessary borrowing.
Those who make this argument seem to reimagine the debt ceiling as some extraneous or superfluous barrier put in place relatively recently to throttle the federal spending process. In truth, the debt ceiling has properly existed in some form since 1917 and was adopted to give the Treasury _more_ , not less, flexibility.
The reason Congress needs to approve federal borrowing is that the Constitution grants this power specifically to Congress in Article I, Section 8: "The Congress shall have Power . . . to borrow Money on the credit of the United States." This is a separate power from appropriations and requires a separate authorization; approval can't be implied from the adoption of a budget resolution or enactment of a spending bill.
If the debt ceiling were eliminated, then the constitutional alternative would be for Congress to expressly authorize specific debt obligations, which is how things worked until the early twentieth century. One way to think about this is to imagine _always_ being at the debt ceiling and needing a small hike every time a new project is approved.
Put differently, short of a constitutional amendment, there's no way to eliminate the _concept_ of a debt ceiling, regardless of whether it's formally recognized as a "debt ceiling." The need for congressional approval of borrowing is an inherent feature of our system.
To abridge Congress's authority here--to allow the executive branch to borrow without limit indefinitely, or even for many years--would be to disregard the separation of powers and defy the Constitution. You can't legally get around this constraint even if Congress decides it's okay. Congress can no more delegate all borrowing authority to the Treasury than it can delegate all spending authority to the Office of Management and Budget.
### Myth #2: Raising the debt ceiling is merely about paying for spending that has already happened
How many times have we heard that raising the debt ceiling is merely about paying for spending that has already happened? If you take just a few minutes to think about it, you can see that this claim is plainly untrue. Yet we hear this one constantly, even from people who should know better.
For example, in a recent Substack [post](https://substack.com/redirect/e185d107-beb5-436f-b0d4-7edfe38479ee?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg), political historian Heather Cox Richardson confidently asserted, "The debt ceiling is not about future spending; future spending is debated when Congress takes up the budget."
Well, she got it half right. Future spending _is_ debated when Congress takes up the budget, but the debt ceiling _is also_ about future spending. More precisely, it's about how to finance spending outlays _that have not yet happened_.
Some of the people who falsely contend that the debt ceiling is unrelated to future spending are employing a sleight of hand by not accounting for all three stages of the spending process: budget authority, obligations, and outlays. They treat spending as having been completed in its entirety after the first two stages.
In reality, the debt ceiling has almost everything to do with future spending. It's in the last stage of the spending process, when money has to go out from the Treasury to satisfy an obligation, that the debt ceiling comes into play. When the debt ceiling is hit, the Treasury can no longer borrow to pay for _upcoming_ outlays (i.e., _future_ spending). If the Treasury has enough cash, no problem. But if the Treasury is short on cash, then it needs either to raise cash or else to have some of those spending obligations go away.
In the short run, Congress can't increase taxes enough to cover the shortfall (nor would this work in the long run--among other problems, annual deficits are way too big). That leaves two main possibilities: Congress can help raise cash for the Treasury by increasing the government's borrowing authority, or Congress can cut expenditures for the Treasury by modifying or rescinding appropriations.
Now, there are some obligations that _must_ be paid. I don't mean _must_ in the sense that any obligee will be able to force the federal government to pay; instead, I mean it in the sense that not paying would wreak havoc on the U.S. financial system. What we're talking about here are primarily debt obligations, in other words, interest on the national debt.
Beyond that, there are obligations that tens of millions of Americans rely on such as Social Security and Medicare. Any sudden, unexpected disruption of this funding would be extremely harmful to many people. (These items are a massive and growing portion of the budget and should be addressed _immediately_ but not _abruptly_.)
But even if you take interest on the debt and all so-called mandatory spending items off the table, that still leaves over one and a half _trillion_ dollars of annual spending available for reconsideration. Remember that spending on an item isn't complete until an outlay occurs.
Some people fall into the trap of the thinking that the government can't backtrack on a particular item of spending once it reaches the outlay stage. It should be apparent why this makes no sense.
First, people do this all the time in everyday life. We place an order online but cancel before the product arrives at our door. We request a service but cancel before it's performed. Sometimes we cancel even after we've received something of value in accordance with a return policy or termination clause.
Second, and more important, these types of cancelations are highly contemplated in the context of the federal government. Our members of Congress are democratically elected. In theory, we have a system of representative government. A past Congress can't bind the current Congress.
Representatives and senators are empowered to re-evaluate prior spending decisions at any stage in the process, even if they're the ones who made those decisions. This notion is so ingrained in our approach to governance that federal contracts ordinarily contain a _termination for convenience_ clause, allowing the government to unilaterally back out of an agreement for any reason or no reason without being held liable for damages.
Now, none of that is to say that changes to spending won't be messy or complicated. For instance, it's possible that a federally funded project has already begun. Stopping it part way may not be pretty. But the consequences of ever-higher borrowing are far uglier, and the point here is that members of Congress who decline to authorize more borrowing without fundamental reforms are simply recognizing what their critics refuse to acknowledge--that the debt ceiling is about _future_ spending.
Finally, a lot of the disruption could be avoided by starting early. There's no reason Congress and the president need to wait until the last second to address any debt ceiling situation. They know months, sometimes years, in advance that the Treasury is going to run out of money on an approximate date. President Biden recently [described](https://substack.com/redirect/d0d1532c-ba8a-4734-96a0-9f5beaa40bbc?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) this state of affairs as a "manufactured crisis." He's right. It's a crisis that presidents and members of Congress from both parties have manufactured time and again by refusing to budget responsibly.
[Upgrade to paid](https://substack..x2EIEYD6xbqHa0qFR-6dCA0GgnfC8Gs9k3z05hIPWZo?&utm_medium=email&utm_source=subscribe-widget&utm_content=120824711)
### Myth #3: Not raising the debt ceiling would cause a "default on America's debt"
It wouldn't be a debt ceiling standoff without round-the-clock fearmongering about a default on the debt. In just two weeks, between May 9 and 22, the Twitter accounts of the [White House](https://substack.com/redirect/75056a99-4352-4997-a2db-3b82ab0dbfe8?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) and [POTUS](https://substack.com/redirect/54b26efb-59c9-464c-a4f7-62d417e3df75?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) tweeted no fewer than 40 times about the possibility of a default, with President Biden referring to it variously as a "[default on America's debt](https://substack.com/redirect/1fbf8e81-3f60-4e47-9303-659e435b29c4?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)," "[default on its debt](https://substack.com/redirect/caf80639-7b22-4240-9fd6-267fa286d0c5?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)," "[default on our debt](https://substack.com/redirect/3fc3f6f4-d9fa-4246-b7b1-7c0684d8c66f?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)," "[default on the national debt](https://substack.com/redirect/7eb4672e-d2ce-499b-8149-a6dd5cf329cf?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)," and "[default on America's debts](https://substack.com/redirect/6c0e65b4-c396-4d42-bbe2-76b2ba2ba2cc?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)."
Republicans in Congress have been alarmists, too, with House Speaker Kevin McCarthy alone tweeting 11 times in two weeks about a potential default.
The truth is there's _zero_ chance of defaulting _on the debt_ absent intentional misconduct by the White House. The reason for this is simple math: There's more than enough money available to the Treasury on any given day to make timely interest payments on the debt. To default _on the debt_ would require deliberate wrongdoing on the part of the executive branch--misfeasance or malfeasance by the president or Treasury secretary that would constitute an impeachable offense.
You can see this by examining [data and projections](https://substack.com/redirect/694b9d98-ce9b-49ab-8dfa-69afbae70f7d?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) from the Congressional Budget Office. In 2023, net interest amounts to just over 10 percent of total outlays. It eats up just under 14 percent of total revenues. Given the government's current finances, there's no circumstance in which it would be unable to pay interest on the debt.
That doesn't mean that interest isn't a problem. It's a growing threat to economic prosperity, made worse each year by successive administrations and Congresses that spend and borrow almost without restraint. Excessive borrowing also fuels inflation, which further exacerbates borrowing costs. If left unchecked, we'll have a lot more to worry about in the decades ahead than defaulting on the debt; the entire socioeconomic system of the United States is at risk.
It seems appropriate here to briefly illustrate why raising taxes will never come close to resolving the debt problem: Analysts estimate that the 2017 tax cuts, which Republicans/Democrats praised/decried for being huge, amounted to around $2 trillion over _10_ years. But the annual deficit is about 10 times larger, approaching $2 trillion over _one_ year. That means that even if we were to treat the entirety of the tax cuts as a revenue reduction to the federal government, they still would account for only slightly more than 10 percent of the yearly deficit.
In short, a tax increase attempting to fill the gap would need to be magnitudes bigger than anything we've ever experienced, and that would prove counterproductive to raising tax revenue, because it would grind the economy to a halt.
Before we leave this section, I'd like to address one more point: Some people would call it a "default" should the Treasury be unable to pay _any_ ongoing obligations.
Although the term "default" is most commonly used with respect to debts, i.e., being unable to repay a loan, I don't object to the use of the looser definition above as long as those employing it are clear they don't mean defaulting _on the debt_. Unfortunately, the president and others have been casually alternating between meanings (sometimes within the same tweet!) for maximum scare effect. And every time they talk about defaulting _on the debt_ , they are misleading the public.
### Myth #4: The president can "invoke the Fourteenth Amendment" to unilaterally raise the debt ceiling
This one might be my favorite myth about the debt ceiling. The claim that the president of the United States can "invoke the Fourteenth Amendment" to unilaterally raise the debt ceiling is so absurd that I hardly know where to start.
Yet it's being parroted as an actual thing in every corner of the politiverse.
On Sunday, following the G7 summit in Hiroshima, Japan, President Biden helped perpetuate this myth, telling reporters, "I'm looking at the Fourteenth Amendment, as to whether or not we have the authority. I think we have the authority."
They do not.
The first sentence of Section 4 of the Fourteenth Amendment reads in full: "The validity of the public debt of the United States, authorized by law, including debts incurred for payment of pensions and bounties for services in suppressing insurrection or rebellion, shall not be questioned."
In other words, the federal government can't repudiate debt held by the public; it's required by law to make good on its _debt_ obligations, such as Treasury bonds, notes, and bills.
Someone somewhere decided that this provision means instead that the president can order the Treasury to borrow money _without_ congressional approval to finance _any_ obligations. This nonsensical interpretation then spread among politicos desperate to justify presidential encroachment on what is indisputably a legislative power.
There's, of course, nothing in the text of the Fourteenth Amendment to warrant such an interpretation. In fact, the text cuts entirely the other way, reaffirming the Constitution's exclusive grant of borrowing authority to Congress.
The clause "authorized by law," which seemingly every person in favor of expansive presidential authority conveniently leaves out when reciting the text, is a reminder that borrowing money on the credit of the United States must be done lawfully. It's an enumerated power of Congress, not a power given to the executive.
Ironically, the people calling for unilateral borrowing by the president are endorsing the creation of public debt _not_ authorized by law. This newly issued debt would then _not_ be secured by the Fourteenth Amendment, because it would _not_ be "public debt of the United States, authorized by law." That means the whole endeavor would not only contravene the separation of powers and violate the Constitution but also be self-defeating.
Worse yet for "invoke the Fourteenth Amendment" proponents, Section 5 of the Fourteenth Amendment triples down on the fact that raising the debt ceiling, even if necessary to comply with Section 4 of the amendment, is a power granted only to Congress, stating: "The Congress shall have power to enforce, by appropriate legislation, the provisions of this article."
The _Congress_. Not the president.
But assuming the president could "invoke the Fourteenth Amendment" to raise the debt ceiling to repay outstanding debt, he'd still have no standing to do so here. That's because the debt ceiling isn't being raised to repay debt; it's being raised to finance upcoming government expenditures _generally_. As discussed above in Myth #3, it's not necessary to raise the debt ceiling to finance debt service, because the Treasury collects more than enough cash to continue making those payments _without any debt ceiling hike_.

Four myths debunked | | | May 23 | | | | | | | | | | | | ---|---|--- Perhaps the most challenging thing about standing on principle as an elected official is that when you take a public position on a controversial item, people frequently judge you not on the basis of your reasoning applied to facts but instead on the basis of whatever narrative has taken hold. I don't really blame most people. It's hard for someone at home to know precisely what's going on in Lansing or Washington or anywhere else for that matter. We're all busy with our own lives. We rely on individuals and organizations we trust--often politicians or media, as much as we hate to admit it--to distill complex public policy matters or legislation into something more accessible. Of course, the politicians and media we rely on routinely take shortcuts themselves. They're also busy and have deadlines to meet. They rarely read bills or dig deep into subjects they're covering, so they too rely on others for summaries and talking points. Given how this all unfolds, especially when partisanship and bias get tossed into the mix, it's hardly surprising that misinformation abounds. When a narrative becomes widespread, it's easy to get sucked into thinking that an alternative view is wrong simply because it's not one believed by most people. If 433 members of Congress say one thing, and Justin Amash and Thomas Massie say another, it's difficult to accept that the lone dissenters might be right. In fact, the main reason I developed the practice of using Facebook to explain all my votes in the Michigan Legislature and Congress was that I knew I couldn't depend on my colleagues or the press to accurately present information to the public. It wasn't just about making sure my constituents knew how I was voting; it was also about making sure we were all working with the same set of facts. It's in that spirit that I bring you this piece titled "The debt ceiling isn't what you think it is." As you may be aware, Treasury Secretary Janet Yellen has been that if Congress fails to raise the debt ceiling by as early as June 1, the United States won't be able to "pay its bills." She's even gone so far as to that "we face economic and financial catastrophe" if Congress doesn't act. The secretary isn't the only one predicting doom if the stalemate between President Joe Biden and congressional Republicans isn't resolved. You can't watch cable news or scroll through your Twitter feed without seeing scores of pundits imploring politicians on Capitol Hill to act immediately to avert the impending crisis. We repeat this drama every year or two, with the president and administration officials proclaiming that the debt ceiling must be hiked or suspended unconditionally and the opposition party in Congress demanding concessions in exchange for their votes to increase the government's borrowing authority. The prevailing position of each party reverses depending on who's in the White House and who controls Congress. Partisans try hard to memory-hole these contradictory performances from leading figures in their party, but they're pretty easy to pull up. | | ---|---|--- | | ---|---|--- What's obvious is that the people we elect don't take the debt ceiling seriously. If they did, then they wouldn't be swapping positions so readily. But it makes perfect sense that they don't take it seriously. They don't take the debt seriously. With rare exception, Democrats and Republicans in Washington aren't concerned about the long-term ramifications of endless borrowing: inflation, high interest rates, economic stagnation. They're looking no further than the next election; they operate in the here and now. What's clear to all of them is that, at the end of any battle over the debt ceiling, a modest deal will be worked out to approve more borrowing, because both parties are addicted to spending. They need that chedda! In the meantime, they're putting on a show for their respective bases, with each side performing its role in this year's production. Regardless of which part they've been assigned this season, Democratic and Republican leaders will make the same insincere assertion to voters: We 're the responsible ones. It's a theater of the absurd, and it's bolstered by countless talking heads on television and social media spreading spurious claims about the debt ceiling. Let's take a look at the most common myths surrounding this perennial spectacle. Myth #1: If Congress repeals the debt ceiling, then the Treasury can simply borrow whatever amount the government needs One of the most common things I'll read or hear whenever the debt ceiling is in the news is that Congress should just repeal the debt ceiling; when the House and Senate vote to approve a budget, they're implicitly voting to approve any necessary borrowing. Those who make this argument seem to reimagine the debt ceiling as some extraneous or superfluous barrier put in place relatively recently to throttle the federal spending process. In truth, the debt ceiling has properly existed in some form since 1917 and was adopted to give the Treasury more , not less, flexibility. The reason Congress needs to approve federal borrowing is that the Constitution grants this power specifically to Congress in Article I, Section 8: "The Congress shall have Power . . . to borrow Money on the credit of the United States." This is a separate power from appropriations and requires a separate authorization; approval can't be implied from the adoption of a budget resolution or enactment of a spending bill. If the debt ceiling were eliminated, then the constitutional alternative would be for Congress to expressly authorize specific debt obligations, which is how things worked until the early twentieth century. One way to think about this is to imagine always being at the debt ceiling and needing a small hike every time a new project is approved. Put differently, short of a constitutional amendment, there's no way to eliminate the concept of a debt ceiling, regardless of whether it's formally recognized as a "debt ceiling." The need for congressional approval of borrowing is an inherent feature of our system. To abridge Congress's authority here--to allow the executive branch to borrow without limit indefinitely, or even for many years--would be to disregard the separation of powers and defy the Constitution. You can't legally get around this constraint even if Congress decides it's okay. Congress can no more delegate all borrowing authority to the Treasury than it can delegate all spending authority to the Office of Management and Budget. Myth #2: Raising the debt ceiling is merely about paying for spending that has already happened How many times have we heard that raising the debt ceiling is merely about paying for spending that has already happened? If you take just a few minutes to think about it, you can see that this claim is plainly untrue. Yet we hear this one constantly, even from people who should know better. For example, in a recent Substack , political historian Heather Cox Richardson confidently asserted, "The debt ceiling is not about future spending; future spending is debated when Congress takes up the budget." Well, she got it half right. Future spending is debated when Congress takes up the budget, but the debt ceiling is also about future spending. More precisely, it's about how to finance spending outlays that have not yet happened. Some of the people who falsely contend that the debt ceiling is unrelated to future spending are employing a sleight of hand by not accounting for all three stages of the spending process: budget authority, obligations, and outlays. They treat spending as having been completed in its entirety after the first two stages. In reality, the debt ceiling has almost everything to do with future spending. It's in the last stage of the spending process, when money has to go out from the Treasury to satisfy an obligation, that the debt ceiling comes into play. When the debt ceiling is hit, the Treasury can no longer borrow to pay for upcoming outlays (i.e., future spending). If the Treasury has enough cash, no problem. But if the Treasury is short on cash, then it needs either to raise cash or else to have some of those spending obligations go away. In the short run, Congress can't increase taxes enough to cover the shortfall (nor would this work in the long run--among other problems, annual deficits are way too big). That leaves two main possibilities: Congress can help raise cash for the Treasury by increasing the government's borrowing authority, or Congress can cut expenditures for the Treasury by modifying or rescinding appropriations. Now, there are some obligations that must be paid. I don't mean must in the sense that any obligee will be able to force the federal government to pay; instead, I mean it in the sense that not paying would wreak havoc on the U.S. financial system. What we're talking about here are primarily debt obligations, in other words, interest on the national debt. Beyond that, there are obligations that tens of millions of Americans rely on such as Social Security and Medicare. Any sudden, unexpected disruption of this funding would be extremely harmful to many people. (These items are a massive and growing portion of the budget and should be addressed immediately but not abruptly.) But even if you take interest on the debt and all so-called mandatory spending items off the table, that still leaves over one and a half trillion dollars of annual spending available for reconsideration. Remember that spending on an item isn't complete until an outlay occurs. Some people fall into the trap of the thinking that the government can't backtrack on a particular item of spending once it reaches the outlay stage. It should be apparent why this makes no sense. First, people do this all the time in everyday life. We place an order online but cancel before the product arrives at our door. We request a service but cancel before it's performed. Sometimes we cancel even after we've received something of value in accordance with a return policy or termination clause. Second, and more important, these types of cancelations are highly contemplated in the context of the federal government. Our members of Congress are democratically elected. In theory, we have a system of representative government. A past Congress can't bind the current Congress. Representatives and senators are empowered to re-evaluate prior spending decisions at any stage in the process, even if they're the ones who made those decisions. This notion is so ingrained in our approach to governance that federal contracts ordinarily contain a termination for convenience clause, allowing the government to unilaterally back out of an agreement for any reason or no reason without being held liable for damages. Now, none of that is to say that changes to spending won't be messy or complicated. For instance, it's possible that a federally funded project has already begun. Stopping it part way may not be pretty. But the consequences of ever-higher borrowing are far uglier, and the point here is that members of Congress who decline to authorize more borrowing without fundamental reforms are simply recognizing what their critics refuse to acknowledge--that the debt ceiling is about future spending. Finally, a lot of the disruption could be avoided by starting early. There's no reason Congress and the president need to wait until the last second to address any debt ceiling situation. They know months, sometimes years, in advance that the Treasury is going to run out of money on an approximate date. President Biden recently this state of affairs as a "manufactured crisis." He's right. It's a crisis that presidents and members of Congress from both parties have manufactured time and again by refusing to budget responsibly. Myth #3: Not raising the debt ceiling would cause a "default on America's debt" It wouldn't be a debt ceiling standoff without round-the-clock fearmongering about a default on the debt. In just two weeks, between May 9 and 22, the Twitter accounts of the and tweeted no fewer than 40 times about the possibility of a default, with President Biden referring to it variously as a "," "," "," "," and "." Republicans in Congress have been alarmists, too, with House Speaker Kevin McCarthy alone tweeting 11 times in two weeks about a potential default. The truth is there's zero chance of defaulting on the debt absent intentional misconduct by the White House. The reason for this is simple math: There's more than enough money available to the Treasury on any given day to make timely interest payments on the debt. To default on the debt would require deliberate wrongdoing on the part of the executive branch--misfeasance or malfeasance by the president or Treasury secretary that would constitute an impeachable offense. You can see this by examining from the Congressional Budget Office. In 2023, net interest amounts to just over 10 percent of total outlays. It eats up just under 14 percent of total revenues. Given the government's current finances, there's no circumstance in which it would be unable to pay interest on the debt. That doesn't mean that interest isn't a problem. It's a growing threat to economic prosperity, made worse each year by successive administrations and Congresses that spend and borrow almost without restraint. Excessive borrowing also fuels inflation, which further exacerbates borrowing costs. If left unchecked, we'll have a lot more to worry about in the decades ahead than defaulting on the debt; the entire socioeconomic system of the United States is at risk. It seems appropriate here to briefly illustrate why raising taxes will never come close to resolving the debt problem: Analysts estimate that the 2017 tax cuts, which Republicans/Democrats praised/decried for being huge, amounted to around $2 trillion over 10 years. But the annual deficit is about 10 times larger, approaching $2 trillion over one year. That means that even if we were to treat the entirety of the tax cuts as a revenue reduction to the federal government, they still would account for only slightly more than 10 percent of the yearly deficit. In short, a tax increase attempting to fill the gap would need to be magnitudes bigger than anything we've ever experienced, and that would prove counterproductive to raising tax revenue, because it would grind the economy to a halt. Before we leave this section, I'd like to address one more point: Some people would call it a "default" should the Treasury be unable to pay any ongoing obligations. Although the term "default" is most commonly used with respect to debts, i.e., being unable to repay a loan, I don't object to the use of the looser definition above as long as those employing it are clear they don't mean defaulting on the debt. Unfortunately, the president and others have been casually alternating between meanings (sometimes within the same tweet!) for maximum scare effect. And every time they talk about defaulting on the debt , they are misleading the public. Myth #4: The president can "invoke the Fourteenth Amendment" to unilaterally raise the debt ceiling This one might be my favorite myth about the debt ceiling. The claim that the president of the United States can "invoke the Fourteenth Amendment" to unilaterally raise the debt ceiling is so absurd that I hardly know where to start. Yet it's being parroted as an actual thing in every corner of the politiverse. On Sunday, following the G7 summit in Hiroshima, Japan, President Biden helped perpetuate this myth, telling reporters, "I'm looking at the Fourteenth Amendment, as to whether or not we have the authority. I think we have the authority." They do not. The first sentence of Section 4 of the Fourteenth Amendment reads in full: "The validity of the public debt of the United States, authorized by law, including debts incurred for payment of pensions and bounties for services in suppressing insurrection or rebellion, shall not be questioned." In other words, the federal government can't repudiate debt held by the public; it's required by law to make good on its debt obligations, such as Treasury bonds, notes, and bills. Someone somewhere decided that this provision means instead that the president can order the Treasury to borrow money without congressional approval to finance any obligations. This nonsensical interpretation then spread among politicos desperate to justify presidential encroachment on what is indisputably a legislative power. There's, of course, nothing in the text of the Fourteenth Amendment to warrant such an interpretation. In fact, the text cuts entirely the other way, reaffirming the Constitution's exclusive grant of borrowing authority to Congress. The clause "authorized by law," which seemingly every person in favor of expansive presidential authority conveniently leaves out when reciting the text, is a reminder that borrowing money on the credit of the United States must be done lawfully. It's an enumerated power of Congress, not a power given to the executive. Ironically, the people calling for unilateral borrowing by the president are endorsing the creation of public debt not authorized by law. This newly issued debt would then not be secured by the Fourteenth Amendment, because it would not be "public debt of the United States, authorized by law." That means the whole endeavor would not only contravene the separation of powers and violate the Constitution but also be self-defeating. Worse yet for "invoke the Fourteenth Amendment" proponents, Section 5 of the Fourteenth Amendment triples down on the fact that raising the debt ceiling, even if necessary to comply with Section 4 of the amendment, is a power granted only to Congress, stating: "The Congress shall have power to enforce, by appropriate legislation, the provisions of this article." The Congress. Not the president. But assuming the president could "invoke the Fourteenth Amendment" to raise the debt ceiling to repay outstanding debt, he'd still have no standing to do so here. That's because the debt ceiling isn't being raised to repay debt; it's being raised to finance upcoming government expenditures generally. As discussed above in Myth #3, it's not necessary to raise the debt ceiling to finance debt service, because the Treasury collects more than enough cash to continue making those payments without any debt ceiling hike. subject: What led me to this moment
disclaimer_text: PAID FOR BY TIM SCOTT FOR AMERICA
body:
Joe Biden and the radical Left have chosen a culture of grievance over greatness. They're promoting victimhood instead of personal responsibility and they're indoctrinating our children to believe we live in an evil country.

| |
---
| | | | [](http://links.reclamingamericanfreedom.com/u/click?_t=5ec072595b1347f09b4bac0de82ea87d&_m=c05608ef6a37442fbd523fd6c8d2b13f&_e=YtnG_qySj15NWDITVIjB5Rvq3gWyoqyqex64lgA18wtgpUF3WcYf8KAtDiIt1xt2-OLTACmu-JZwxJFsmKHIMdKlFHjfOHtHcp3u65q83dtUT1I_YSoJxC0fJR8PPfberEqp-6BK2TS7QeBoFdqxdueVx_jO0Mm62ojjmchmV15JWkGw5-I9CHy560uzAAMUtKYs5ix3NdJa_XY1nUXGDzDdsrrOzog0O04m3C1MpDS2iVVvlGvBWoxiIN_4sBSzwOiLRlAzMJXUvULltAiO7-TmO-USTwMCySZ3SIqB8Wi_IBbABEL0Gd2_07XZOJdlCNwJtfuibXWBnenV1g4jxLO_MMFvBQ8-7NtfJkU75akmR4Ue0Uoo4ODfiXaWeTa4_g5bGiZSiup50SuVpd-WFjw-h-5uetTdmNQNd0wdwVtaSaBiG4YstZKLCg69y5Y4 "TIM SCOTT - FAITH IN AMERICA")
---

Friend,

I was raised in a single-parent household in North Charleston, South Carolina. My mom worked 16-hour days to keep food on the table and a roof over our heads. I got my first job, changing oil at a gas station, when I was 13. And I wouldn't ask for it to be any other way.

Charleston is home to me. It's where I grew up, it's where I learned to play football, and it's where I learned to be a man of faith and conservative values.

It's all of these things that I learned in Charleston that have led me to this exact moment: **I 'm officially running for President of the United States.**

---
| | | [SUPPORT ME HERE](http://links.reclamingamericanfreedom.com/u/click?_t=5ec072595b1347f09b4bac0de82ea87d&_m=c05608ef6a37442fbd523fd6c8d2b13f&_e=YtnG_qySj15NWDITVIjB5Rvq3gWyoqyqex64lgA18wtgpUF3WcYf8KAtDiIt1xt2-OLTACmu-JZwxJFsmKHIMdKlFHjfOHtHcp3u65q83dtUT1I_YSoJxC0fJR8PPfberEqp-6BK2TS7QeBoFdqxdueVx_jO0Mm62ojjmchmV15JWkGw5-I9CHy560uzAAMUtKYs5ix3NdJa_XY1nUXGDzDdsrrOzog0O04m3C1MpDS2iVVvlGvBWoxiIN_4sBSzwOiLRlAzMJXUvULltAiO7-TmO-USTwMCySZ3SIqB8WgbakPSHSrITb34bnPLeUc4iLhHzKHkJd8igW1cANWPojBX8tGJJRwpDVR0fEYK4K1i7QaP9xZXk2mHVaCPYSJPgPBKuokOyprAuG2rcjlmjPiGQX40hFQkwDW5VqQEPwdIEp1bh7uDxlY8PkQFUi7g "SUPPORT ME HERE")
---

Today, our country is being tested. Once again, our divisions run deep and the threat to our future is real. Joe Biden and the radical Left have chosen a culture of grievance over greatness. They're promoting victimhood instead of personal responsibility and they're indoctrinating our children to believe we live in an evil country.

And all too often when they get called out for their failures they weaponize race to divide us... to hold onto their power. When I fought back against their liberal agenda they called me a prop. A token. Because I disrupt their narrative. I threaten their control.

And that's how I know I'm doing something right. And that's why I will continue to fight because I believe America's best days are ahead of us.

**_[So, Friend, will you please be one of the first donors to my Presidential campaign? I'm going to need every single person who believes we can get America BACK ON TRACK and I'm really counting on you to chip in to kick this off. I hope to see your name as a fellow American that has faith in this country.](http://links.reclamingamericanfreedom.com/u/click?_t=5ec072595b1347f09b4bac0de82ea87d&_m=c05608ef6a37442fbd523fd6c8d2b13f&_e=YtnG_qySj15NWDITVIjB5Rvq3gWyoqyqex64lgA18wtgpUF3WcYf8KAtDiIt1xt2-OLTACmu-JZwxJFsmKHIMdKlFHjfOHtHcp3u65q83dtUT1I_YSoJxC0fJR8PPfberEqp-6BK2TS7QeBoFdqxdueVx_jO0Mm62ojjmchmV15JWkGw5-I9CHy560uzAAMUtKYs5ix3NdJa_XY1nUXGDzDdsrrOzog0O04m3C1MpDS2iVVvlGvBWoxiIN_4sBSzwOiLRlAzMJXUvULltAiO7-TmO-USTwMCySZ3SIqB8WhnWQyT8oLO3CE7667E6x-1OHeZPn4C2SnEB8619P8zDUdmAQZL1DnWZsUTlLeJetcBtz4SVZOWxG-WJl4l2B-rkThDsvJzey-McAIHMJCwpcZuaYed69ejxplo9k2O9nMWOCxAw0akSEMpg404chUr "So, Friend, will you please be one of the first donors to my Presidential campaign? I’m going to need every single person who believes we can get America BACK ON TRACK and I’m really counting on you to chip in to kick this off. I hope to see your name as a fellow American that has faith in this country.")_**

Friend, I believe our country is hungry for positive, conservative leadership.

It is time for us to restore hope, create opportunities, and protect America. **It is time for us to be proud to be Americans and proud of our president.**

**_[That's why I announced I am running for President of the United States.](http://links.reclamingamericanfreedom.com/u/click?_t=5ec072595b1347f09b4bac0de82ea87d&_m=c05608ef6a37442fbd523fd6c8d2b13f&_e=YtnG_qySj15NWDITVIjB5Rvq3gWyoqyqex64lgA18wtgpUF3WcYf8KAtDiIt1xt2eta2EUotqchV02XUEmktLJx-1i69b-xwf9YHrWQhMkL8gLaRB6jpw2Oxmr6WZ0sIFHWJdpHqP4rmEteAMeDId1Lrxsy6IMux7IJn0xqjieF_Hy-CRC9DJYHbN6hGSzNsHzetQJ-vRhiQsN9TJkounxIpqfEjVQLNJb-DafIfpeqWIL-z8ENF-RJ4-iimOwQgjN8wrFhYLqVbPS9B3mKM9nvSZ2T_7Jjw_Zu8oopew5kc0fkk76DlxlS7sycob0vihIjSWbaZjuA7RqMolKlF5uO-ncuZvPhEnQ3w_YnSgYH7_CKGqDkZJ6jnDg-h__M0mK7V7wMfp7wtmQB7NiN0zIoE3Ga-TDv0MsElFo9bRHuEk3ur_bAoXqzxKFG2TUVBZBxixj6uXSqvWOAZJkYbuA%3D%3D "That’s why I announced I am running for President of the United States.")_**

Charleston is home to me. It's where I grew up, it's where I learned to play football, and it's where I learned to be a man of faith and conservative values. And all too often when they get called out for their failures they weaponize race to divide us... to hold onto their power. When I fought back against their liberal agenda they called me a prop. A token. Because I disrupt their narrative. I threaten their control. And that's how I know I'm doing something right. And that's why I will continue to fight because I believe America's best days are ahead of us. "So, Friend, will you please be one of the first donors to my Presidential campaign? name: =?utf-8?Q?Caleb=20Heimlich?=
subject: Join us and Spokane County Treasurer Michael Baumgartner
disclaimer_text: Paid for by the Washington State Republican Party
body:
Come see Michael at our Spokane Heart of the Party and our 46th Annual Dinner! | | | [ ](https://wsrp.us2.list-manage.com/track/click?u=be1a8605f1118163852707f35&id=cbdaf301a7&e=6a281f566d)
---
| |
---
| Dear Peter,

I am thrilled to announce that Spokane County Treasurer Michael Baumgartner, a steadfast Republican leader with an impressive record of public service, will be joining us at two of our upcoming events: As the Master of Ceremonies at our 46th Annual Dinner in Bellevue, and as a special guest at our 5th Annual 'Heart of the Party' Reception in Spokane! Before becoming the Spokane County Treasurer, Michael Baumgartner served as a Washington State Senator from the 6th Legislative District, most notably serving as the Vice-Chair of the important Ways and Means Committee.

Before his time in elected office, Michael was a US State Department Officer in Iraq and a counternarcotics advisor in Afghanistan. His global perspective, formed by extensive travel to over 80 countries, makes him an invaluable asset to our party. Michael is a public servant through and through and we appreciate his strong dedication to our state, country, and Republican Party.

I hope you are just as excited as I am to join fellow Republicans and Michael at one, or both, of these upcoming events! Please click on the links below to find more details and register today!
---
|
---
| 🌟 **46th Annual Washington State Republican Party Dinner:** Michael emceed our 2022 Annual Dinner and was such a crowd favorite that we had to bring him back again this year! If you missed last years' dinner - be sure to come to this one! This event promises to be one of our best yet as we honor the outstanding leadership of Congresswoman Cathy McMorris Rodgers, and welcome Congressman Burgess Owens (UT-04) as a keynote speaker.

_**Event Details:**_
**Date:** Friday, October 27th
**Time:** 6 PM | VIP & General Receptions, 7 PM | Dinner
**Location:** Hyatt Regency Bellevue, Bellevue, WA
**Tickets:**[Click here!](https://wsrp.us2.list-manage.com/track/click?u=be1a8605f1118163852707f35&id=e8e226960c&e=6a281f566d)
---
| [Get Tickets Now! [46th Annual Dinner]](https://wsrp.us2.list-manage.com/track/click?u=be1a8605f1118163852707f35&id=996e09b60a&e=6a281f566d "Get Tickets Now! \[46th Annual Dinner\]")
---
| 🌟 **Heart of the Party - Spokane:** We invite you to join us, alongside Spokane County Treasurer Michael Baumgartner and Congresswoman Cathy McMorris Rodgers, at our 5th Annual 'Heart of the Party' Reception in Spokane. This event is your opportunity to engage with local elected officials, candidates, and fellow Republicans from the Spokane Area at the Beacon Hill Event Center.

_**Event Details:**_
**Date:** Tuesday, June 27
**Time:** 6 PM - 8 PM (VIP Roundtable at 5 PM)
**Location:** Beacon Hill Event Center, Spokane, WA
**Tickets:** [Click here!](https://wsrp.us2.list-manage.com/track/click?u=be1a8605f1118163852707f35&id=35ce8b8a79&e=6a281f566d)
---
| [Get Tickets Now! [Spokane Heart of the Party]](https://wsrp.us2.list-manage.com/track/click?u=be1a8605f1118163852707f35&id=655ca9fdea&e=6a281f566d "Get Tickets Now! \[Spokane Heart of the Party\]")
---
| [Join us at a Heart of the Party Reception Near You!](https://wsrp.us2.list-manage.com/track/click?u=be1a8605f1118163852707f35&id=2023f5adb8&e=6a281f566d "Join us at a Heart of the Party Reception Near You!")
---
| | These events give us an opportunity to celebrate what makes our party great, but they are also very important to helping fund the important work of the WSRP as we try to maintain one of the largest field programs in the country, reach new voters with our messaging, and implement new programs like Ballot Collection that will bring our party to victory this year and next. Don't miss this chance to connect, celebrate, and work together towards a stronger Washington State.

In partnership with you,

Caleb Heimlich
Chairman
Washington State Republican Party
---
| | [View this email in your browser](https://mailchi.mp/wsrp/michael-baumgartner-announcement-1341104?e=6a281f566d)
---
| _Copyright © 2023 Washington State Republican Party, All rights reserved._
Your interest in the Washington State Republican Party, National Republican Committee or Washington state Republican Candidates

**Our mailing address is:**
Washington State Republican Party11811 NE 1st St Suite 306Bellevue, WA 98005
[Add us to your address book](https://wsrp.us2.list-manage.com/vcard?u=be1a8605f1118163852707f35&id=4f90cbb438)

Want to change how you receive these emails?
You can [update your preferences](https://wsrp.us2.list-manage.com/profile?u=be1a8605f1118163852707f35&id=4f90cbb438&e=6a281f566d&c=ebd9fded0d) or [unsubscribe from this list](https://wsrp.us2.list-manage.com/unsubscribe?u=be1a8605f1118163852707f35&id=4f90cbb438&e=6a281f566d&c=ebd9fded0d).

name: Elizabeth Warren
subject: We should eliminate the debt ceiling altogether. It serves no purpose.
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Warren for Senate
body:
Republicans are using this moment to hold our economy — and livelihoods of millions — hostage. | | | [ ](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/63722648/407769033/-1964596891?ms=em-header&refcode=EMZ13Y_GOP_E_20230523_V1S3&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQMDgvMS84NzkzMiIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICJiOTQzMTgzZC1iZmY5LWVkMTEtOTA3Yy0wMDIyNDgzMmViNzMiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=SchIkGuVLQ38llO2Vk08sE0O6Do2biAijbr52ZQkwaY=&emci=881efd35-bff9-ed11-907c-00224832eb73&emdi=b943183d-bff9-ed11-907c-00224832eb73&ceid=9027908)
---
You know, I’d like to think that politicians run for office to help people. But the GOP doesn’t seem to be interested in that. They tout how they fight for the everyman. But that isn’t really true — at all. As we continue to plummet towards the June 1 deadline to raise the debt ceiling, the House GOP’s demands have shown that they’d rather take food and health care away from people who are struggling than take $1 more in taxes from a billionaire. They are threatening to push our nation off an economic cliff — and throw millions of hard-working Americans out of their jobs — if Democrats don’t agree to slashing programs like nutrition assistance, rental assistance, Meals on Wheels, and veterans’ health care. And at the same time, Republicans across the country are passing bills that take rights away from trans people, make it harder to access FDA-approved abortion medication nationwide, and control what our educators can teach at school. **I am fighting back with everything I’ve got to make sure they can’t win — and I’m going to keep laying out big, bold plans that actually lift up working families and protect our values. But if Republicans gain more power, they’d have more free reign to pass their extremist agenda and block progress on the plans this movement is fighting for.** [_**Can you pitch in $15 today to fund my re-election campaign and support our progressive movement? A contribution helps show that our movement for big, structural change is stronger than their movement for extremist, harmful regression.**_](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/63722649/407769034/-551446403?amount=15&refcode=EMZ13Y_GOP_E_20230523_V1S3&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQMDgvMS84NzkzMiIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICJiOTQzMTgzZC1iZmY5LWVkMTEtOTA3Yy0wMDIyNDgzMmViNzMiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=SchIkGuVLQ38llO2Vk08sE0O6Do2biAijbr52ZQkwaY=) | _If you've saved payment info with ActBlue Express, your donation will process automatically:_

>
I was saying last year that we needed to raise the debt ceiling before the Republican wrecking-ball crew took over the House. In fact, I suggested we should eliminate the debt ceiling altogether, because it serves no purpose — Congress already signs off on spending through an entirely separate process.
But here we are. And Republicans are using this moment to hold our economy — and livelihoods of millions — hostage.
This isn’t the first time we’ve seen such drastic views from Republicans, and it won’t be the last. From the future of our climate to the survival of our democracy, so much is at stake if Republicans seize more power, and we must stop them.
And we can — with a grassroots movement powered by grassroots donors. Every single donation helps strengthen this movement so we can push back against the GOP and pass our plans for big, structural change. And every single donation brings us closer to hitting our critical fundraising goals we need to hit in order to run this campaign.

name: HER Time Abortion Alert
subject: Federal judges spewing anti-abortion propaganda
disclaimer_text: Paid for by the HER Time PAC and not authorized by any candidate or candidate’s committee
body:
|
# [](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/63714213/407740153/-2095412496?refcode=AU_HTP_FR_GEN_GEN_AC_20230523_PM1_V1_S3&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQNDYvMS8xMDI1NDMiLA0KICAiRGlzdHJpYnV0aW9uVW5pcXVlSWQiOiAiNGRhODRlNjEtYTBmOS1lZDExLTkwN2MtMDAyMjQ4MzJlYjczIiwNCiAgIkVtYWlsQWRkcmVzcyI6ICJkcHdpbGxpczY3QGdtYWlsLmNvbSINCn0%3D&hmac=EnIWPGJy54ILQRjWIQ5Y1Rv5sE_odccANqPkO8WYEoU=)
---
Peter, Last week, three federal judges heard the latest arguments in the mifepristone case, the case currently working its way through our judicial system that could potentially cut off access to the most common abortion method for the entire country. _The hearing could not have gone worse._ The three-judge panel spewed anti-abortion propaganda, including the lie that mifepristone makes folks more likely to need emergency care, and that the FDA only approved mifepristone under “expedited review” — neither of which claims are true. These three judges are all Republican appointees, and are all known to be hostile to abortion. That’s why we’re preparing for the worst, and then an eventual appeal to the U.S. Supreme Court. **[We don’t know when this decision is coming or what exactly it will say, but we need to be ready. Can you pitch in just $3 today to our Rapid Response Mifepristone Fund so we can stock up the resources we need to fight back?](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/63714214/407740154/-1193769910?amount=3&refcode=AU_HTP_FR_GEN_GEN_AC_20230523_PM1_V1_S3&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQNDYvMS8xMDI1NDMiLA0KICAiRGlzdHJpYnV0aW9uVW5pcXVlSWQiOiAiNGRhODRlNjEtYTBmOS1lZDExLTkwN2MtMDAyMjQ4MzJlYjczIiwNCiAgIkVtYWlsQWRkcmVzcyI6ICJkcHdpbGxpczY3QGdtYWlsLmNvbSINCn0%3D&hmac=EnIWPGJy54ILQRjWIQ5Y1Rv5sE_odccANqPkO8WYEoU=)** _If you've saved your payment information with ActBlue Express, your donation will go through immediately:_ | | [CHIP IN $3 NOW](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/63714215/407740155/-1193769910?express_lane=true&amount=3&refcode=AU_HTP_FR_GEN_GEN_AC_20230523_PM1_V1_S3&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQNDYvMS8xMDI1NDMiLA0KICAiRGlzdHJpYnV0aW9uVW5pcXVlSWQiOiAiNGRhODRlNjEtYTBmOS1lZDExLTkwN2MtMDAyMjQ4MzJlYjczIiwNCiAgIkVtYWlsQWRkcmVzcyI6ICJkcHdpbGxpczY3QGdtYWlsLmNvbSINCn0%3D&hmac=EnIWPGJy54ILQRjWIQ5Y1Rv5sE_odccANqPkO8WYEoU=)

name: The Flyover
subject: We deliver the facts. You reach your own conclusion.
body:
Half the American people believe national news organizations intend to mislead, misinform, and push their bias and opinions on you and the American public.

| [](https://links.conservativelibertywire.com/u/click?_t=959d0fa54cad47baa4213176314ae733&_m=a249a3d7ba0f41a6b5be525b02d60bd8&_e=1jJ2p5jh3jLld41QWUjjzNa9OepedKToIeSRhbdvkbhMclnoHZx-55L4CgL2S3ddUarpGBLTW6CBgKgyDoUGiq5f837UURv4sF9jarcqXszn9MP7K_WrqNq-6c0lL4hlX3ULV86GRifi2phBvmaoBZFzWeIpvLFX-M-0guFDrZTpXL0H4gwmMfcQO07JYk74Lki7uKP_bQY2Dtd39ZuOyw%3D%3D)
* * *
---
A massive shift is happening in the American Media. The corporate elite news media has lost the trust of the American people.

**New York Times huge layoffs...

BuzzFeed News out of business...

CNN closing divisions and selling off parts...

CBS downsizing...

Vice News out of business...

MSNBC off the rails...

Washington Post sacking staff...**

name: Greg Casar
subject: The latest on default crisis
disclaimer_text: PAID FOR BY GREG CASAR FOR CONGRESS
body:
[](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/63716073/407741472/1120393829?refcode=em-wrapper&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQMzEvMS85NDI5NyIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICJlNjRhOTJiZC1hMmY5LWVkMTEtOTA3Yy0wMDIyNDgzMmViNzMiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=RHdMu8Db-KKmhE5G6qj4xbxqtiycrSfMJpU6_50IZJE=&emci=e44a92bd-a2f9-ed11-907c-00224832eb73&emdi=e64a92bd-a2f9-ed11-907c-00224832eb73&ceid=6830379)
Peter,

**As I write this email, my Republican colleagues are threatening to plunge the entire U.S. into default and economic catastrophe.

This is the most consequential moment of my time so far in Congress. Here’s the latest:**

The Treasury Department says that as soon as June 1, the United States could default unless Congress does what it has always done: simply pay our bills. Not paying our bills could trigger a recession where millions of Americans lose their jobs.

This is entirely avoidable. Congress could vote today to keep paying our bills - as we have more than 100 times before under Republican and Democratic presidents. But right-wing extremists have decided to use this deadline to extort Democrats into gutting programs that help working class families — all to secure further tax breaks for Republican special interest donors.

But we aren’t playing their games. I won’t support a bill that throws working families under the bus to pay the GOP’s ransom note. All we need are FIVE Republicans (of over 200) to agree that they will simply allow the President to pay the bills. So far, none of them have signed up.

**Unfortunately, some extreme Republicans don’t mind causing an economic catastrophe because they think that’s politically advantageous for them. They’d just blame it all on President Biden. That’s why I joined dozens of my colleagues in supporting the President’s right to invoke the 14th Amendment of the Constitution to pay our bills and avoid economic default, if it comes down to it. We won’t accept a default, and we won’t accept extreme cuts to programs like Medicaid.**

We can’t allow far-right extremists to hold the global economy hostage so they can hurt working Americans. **[If you agree, chip in $10 to have our backs in this critical moment. Your support means so much in this fight.](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/63716074/407741473/1386799382?nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQMzEvMS85NDI5NyIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICJlNjRhOTJiZC1hMmY5LWVkMTEtOTA3Yy0wMDIyNDgzMmViNzMiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=RHdMu8Db-KKmhE5G6qj4xbxqtiycrSfMJpU6_50IZJE=)**

subject: let me be 100% honest with you
disclaimer_text: Paid for by TEAM SCALISE, a joint fundraising committee authorized by and to benefit SCALISE FOR CONGRESS and EYE OF THE TIGER PAC
body:
\
If you know anything about me, you know I don't beat around the bush.

| |

---
| | | This is Jim Jordan.

If you know anything about me, you know I don't beat around the bush. If I'm worried about something, you'll hear about it. Immediately.

So let me be 100% honest with you right now.

I just spoke with Majority Leader Steve Scalise and he told me we're in quite a bind: **1)** _Inside Elections_ released their first set of House Ratings and they have us TIED with the Democrats for control of the House **2)** The latest FEC reports show Democrats OUTRAISED Republicans by $12,000,000+... **3)** _**[We still need fifteen-hundred strong Conservatives to make a small donation right away or our Republican Majority will be in SERIOUS JEOPARDY.](https://links.conservativesgive.com/u/click?_t=959d0fa54cad47baa4213176314ae733&_m=61d44af9684a46869509003d91571acb&_e=aUHtBdnGDM_UcJTTlXNuI5GuWoBX8fxq3Z2wwOLKMSI-tCGTIdXBasjn1aO8j1kyhER7H3ZBrPWiqfa4m_ZLdkkWfB-zkMltrMhMKL1vm6uuHtAnHl0_40xofGUr_lijTp8WflOB-eFxWHkVPSuIeYIDfwtJLFMMeHbtg5m6tQHCKZ7msjtS2VVLbgKUvlBtcy7M1Op700AmaavAqTP9dnIbl21lTyi-I0OzKqxoJcYxyNPq0HQlBmIcAOV7WnCbsQMD0poAx-Jn3zfcNn6YpbVmEaAiMul60JIJgQe2W90upki6xkneBYVwa2ob-fyDvDGBWinqfIF_6eTaYRht0Pygi6cuWVbqZK2vEFCCzC_33vT72jxeFJ5RAh25pNANIhZOT5qXWmp_mMV7aYxuXbmsm4iwxGdgHMZisg6qVpsUURWgYe9lVdsNG5V391sU "We still need fifteen-hundred strong Conservatives to make a small donation right away or our Republican Majority will be in SERIOUS JEOPARDY.")**_
---
| | | [I'M PERSONALLY ASKING: CAN YOU HELP?](https://links.conservativesgive.com/u/click?_t=959d0fa54cad47baa4213176314ae733&_m=61d44af9684a46869509003d91571acb&_e=aUHtBdnGDM_UcJTTlXNuI5GuWoBX8fxq3Z2wwOLKMSI-tCGTIdXBasjn1aO8j1kyhER7H3ZBrPWiqfa4m_ZLdkkWfB-zkMltrMhMKL1vm6uuHtAnHl0_40xofGUr_lijTp8WflOB-eFxWHkVPSuIeYIDfwtJLFMMeHbtg5m6tQHCKZ7msjtS2VVLbgKUvlBtcy7M1Op700AmaavAqTP9dnIbl21lTyi-I0OzKqxoJcYxyNPq0HQlBmIcAOV7WnCbsQMD0poAx-Jn3zfcNn6YpbVmEaAiMul60JIJgQe2W92EAda8of4F_gAfhcrbPihJtUD1B4zXgTlElhHQA6nRRc1ddDrC4zRDuzdVjPDFZF72TxHGTg98q1CTuk6HUkDO_DyYC6nh-TMmXNfDmywEDL1ZgLxtAgAe78OrUGPdBgttYUOkwrZ9gpf3GjI0G50i "I’M PERSONALLY ASKING: CAN YOU HELP?")
---
Listen, House Democrats exploited every ounce of their power to cover for Joe Biden and misled the public during every crisis they created together.

_If they outraise us and manage to destroy our Majority, the American People will never get ..._
* **ANSWERS** on Biden's weak response to Chinese aggression
* **ANSWERS** on President Trump's corrupt indictment
* **ANSWERS** on how Big Tech interfered with our elections
* **ANSWERS** on Democrats colluding with Big Tech to silence critics
* **ANSWERS** on Biden officials treating parents as domestic terrorists

The American People deserve answers, but getting them depends entirely on Republicans holding the House Majority. If we lose our Majority, we will lose all of the progress we made.

We need the support of our grassroots donors to prevail. **Without it, we might as well throw in the towel.**

_**[As I write this, we're falling critically behind the Democrats' fundraising efforts. I'm counting on you to make a small donation to help us catch up before our critical deadline.](https://links.conservativesgive.com/u/click?_t=959d0fa54cad47baa4213176314ae733&_m=61d44af9684a46869509003d91571acb&_e=aUHtBdnGDM_UcJTTlXNuI5GuWoBX8fxq3Z2wwOLKMSI-tCGTIdXBasjn1aO8j1kyhER7H3ZBrPWiqfa4m_ZLdkkWfB-zkMltrMhMKL1vm6uuHtAnHl0_40xofGUr_lijTp8WflOB-eFxWHkVPSuIeYIDfwtJLFMMeHbtg5m6tQHCKZ7msjtS2VVLbgKUvlBtcy7M1Op700AmaavAqTP9dnIbl21lTyi-I0OzKqxoJcYxyNPq0HQlBmIcAOV7WnCbsQMD0poAx-Jn3zfcNn6YpbVmEaAiMul60JIJgQe2W90edQ2aV4-2XxSIWuiW-Oop1rrwDSbqykEN1E7KmMicYrHWOlplIjWlMfmDAJha5zRM5lAn1nl4DRHCCWdq70WXiX8NaJ9iHm9EXzUB_onxTHrE7dfETfLUyFR-BEYe7vfsRb7A5GHINSFy_lpgRSaA "As I write this, we’re falling critically behind the Democrats’ fundraising efforts. I’m counting on you to make a small donation to help us catch up before our critical deadline.")**_

Time is of the essence and I'm really counting on you.

For America,

Jim Jordan

I just spoke with Majority Leader Steve Scalise and he told me we're in quite a bind: 1) Inside Elections released their first set of House Ratings and they have us TIED with the Democrats for control of the House 2) The latest FEC reports show Democrats OUTRAISED Republicans by $12,000,000+... 3) "We still need fifteen-hundred strong Conservatives to make a small donation right away or our Republican Majority will be in SERIOUS JEOPARDY." | | | "I’M PERSONALLY ASKING: CAN YOU HELP?" Listen, House Democrats exploited every ounce of their power to cover for Joe Biden and misled the public during every crisis they created together. If they outraise us and manage to destroy our Majority, the American People will never get ... ANSWERS on Biden's weak response to Chinese aggression ANSWERS on President Trump's corrupt indictment ANSWERS on how Big Tech interfered with our elections ANSWERS on Democrats colluding with Big Tech to silence critics ANSWERS on Biden officials treating parents as domestic terrorists The American People deserve answers, but getting them depends entirely on Republicans holding the House Majority. If we lose our Majority, we will lose all of the progress we made. We need the support of our grassroots donors to prevail. Without it, we might as well throw in the towel. "As I write this, we’re falling critically behind the Democrats’ fundraising efforts. I’m counting on you to make a small donation to help us catch up before our critical deadline." Time is of the essence and I'm really counting on you. For America, Jim Jordan | "Jim Jordan" | | | "I’M PERSONALLY ASKING: CAN YOU HELP?" | | Paid for by TEAM SCALISE, a joint fundraising committee authorized by and to benefit SCALISE FOR CONGRESS and EYE OF THE TIGER PAC. name: Jon Tester
subject: When I was a kid, I lost three fingers on my left hand while working in our family’s butcher shop.
disclaimer_text: PAID FOR BY TAMMY BALDWIN FOR SENATE
body:
[ https://act.tammybaldwin.com/go/67?t=1001&akid=320%2E35263%2EBq39RI ]TAMMY BALDWIN FOR SENATE

[ https://act.tammybaldwin.com/go/2155?c0=tbe-em-tester.Z0aSwr&refcode=AU_STB_FR_TAN_GEN_AC_20230523_PM1_V1_S1&t=1004&akid=320%2E35263%2EBq39RI ]Jon Tester

Peter, can I tell you a little story about me?

I grew up on my family’s farm and it’s still home today. That explains a
lot about me.

When I was a kid, I lost three fingers on my left hand while working in
our family’s butcher shop. I don’t even remember putting my hand there —
but I can damn sure remember rushing to a local doctor.

Here’s why I’m telling you this: just like my $12 flattop haircut from my
local barbershop back home and my collection of dirt-stained t-shirts, my
background is a big part of the reason I remain grounded and focused amid
the partisan politics and nonsense happening in our government.

With all the chaos in Washington (especially now), you need somebody who
will fight to protect our public lands, create good-paying jobs, and
ensure rural communities have hospitals within driving distance in case of
an emergency.

I’m pretty different from most people who end up in the U.S. Senate. I’m
just a dirt farmer trying to do what’s right for the people in my state
and across the country.

[ https://act.tammybaldwin.com/go/2155?c0=tbe-em-tester.Z0aSwr&refcode=AU_STB_FR_TAN_GEN_AC_20230523_PM1_V1_S1&t=1005&akid=320%2E35263%2EBq39RI ]So here’s the part where I ask: Can you become a Founding Donor of my
campaign and Tammy Baldwin’s to help us block Mitch McConnell from
retaking the Senate?

If you've saved your payment information with ActBlue Express, your split
donation to Jon Tester and Tammy Baldwin will go through immediately:

[ https://act.tammybaldwin.com/go/2150?c0=tbe-em-tester.xY94AM&refcode=AU_STB_FR_TAN_GEN_AC_20230523_PM1_V1_S1&t=1006&akid=320%2E35263%2EBq39RI ]CHIP IN $5 NOW

[ https://act.tammybaldwin.com/go/2151?c0=tbe-em-tester.5zk3A_&refcode=AU_STB_FR_TAN_GEN_AC_20230523_PM1_V1_S1&t=1007&akid=320%2E35263%2EBq39RI ]CHIP IN $10 NOW

[ https://act.tammybaldwin.com/go/2152?c0=tbe-em-tester.B4djTN&refcode=AU_STB_FR_TAN_GEN_AC_20230523_PM1_V1_S1&t=1008&akid=320%2E35263%2EBq39RI ]CHIP IN $25 NOW

[ https://act.tammybaldwin.com/go/2153?c0=tbe-em-tester.bxtC9B&refcode=AU_STB_FR_TAN_GEN_AC_20230523_PM1_V1_S1&t=1009&akid=320%2E35263%2EBq39RI ]CHIP IN $50 NOW

[ https://act.tammybaldwin.com/go/2154?c0=tbe-em-tester.71esfR&refcode=AU_STB_FR_TAN_GEN_AC_20230523_PM1_V1_S1&t=1010&akid=320%2E35263%2EBq39RI ]CHIP IN $100 NOW

[ https://act.tammybaldwin.com/go/2155?c0=tbe-em-tester.Z0aSwr&refcode=AU_STB_FR_TAN_GEN_AC_20230523_PM1_V1_S1&t=1011&akid=320%2E35263%2EBq39RI ]ANOTHER AMOUNT

You don’t need ten fingers to know right from wrong and you can bet I’m
going to spend every single day trying to do right by working families,
farmers, students, and veterans here in Montana and across the country.

I appreciate your support, and anything you can pitch in today will go a
long way for our grassroots campaign.

Thanks again,

Jon

P.O. BOX 696 MADISON, WI 53701

[ https://act.tammybaldwin.com/go/67?t=1002&akid=320%2E35263%2EBq39RI ]www.tammybaldwin.com | [ mailto:INFO@tammybaldwin.com ]INFO@TAMMYBALDWIN.COM | [ https://act.tammybaldwin.com/go/68?t=1003&akid=320%2E35263%2EBq39RI ]Privacy Policy

Friend,

**I 'm running for President of the United States!**

_I believe we live in the greatest country on God 's green Earth, Friend.
I believe that America can do for ANYONE what she has done for me.
And I believe I am living proof that America is a Land of Opportunity, not a land of oppression._

**_[If you agree with me, then I ask you to please chip in anything you can afford to help me restore faith in America and get our country back on track.](http://links.ourdefenseofamerica.com/u/click?_t=aa3ef0db03454a26b650c9ca7e919386&_m=1dfb68f43e594636b05eb372ef2b883d&_e=2dWzBPbGvtccb912kmteSnIoCV_RkPzq_ewbRKEBu-zV2ZRob1gKgeDPJH5wlEGwbcmvYMnbDSLMvJgB1HW_mSOXQj3Q79o9q6oZTipSziwhD68Wf1eZtxlb4nOI-2YNJa4-RtBOg1Ewq_EHae0w6ng3SjM71oLWv9eWNmWumM9wLACln4RdgofaOPXdJps4QBqU3uO-P2hA84TjU_5yfSrkYswxnzgaT5TFDslRcImj0Suk4fy5QKpbsbmQOU1wCRawKsrvG3eMqWjzFtzK_msWgIcp2j29vwTSpx4IDGM1-vhd2osOE7bcI5b43zyNEyoBOhtfMGzrAnlfC84Nt4DWzNnIyT3OUpVNkPIaC9tSfKa_NweM3onfuviv7rpO9rIeFFrMzVdQMpgwFtmkmA%3D%3D "If you agree with me, then I ask you to please chip in anything you can afford to help me restore faith in America and get our country back on track.")_**

No longer will we allow our kids to grow up in a culture **where everyone is a victim.**
No longer will we allow our kids to be **indoctrinated,** able bodied people to **avoid work,** and violent criminals to **roam our streets with no repercussions.**
No longer will we ignore the **necessity of individual responsibility.**

I'm determined to tell the truth, Friend, but I need your help to do it.

**_[Will you please chip in before midnight?](http://links.ourdefenseofamerica.com/u/click?_t=aa3ef0db03454a26b650c9ca7e919386&_m=1dfb68f43e594636b05eb372ef2b883d&_e=2dWzBPbGvtccb912kmteSnIoCV_RkPzq_ewbRKEBu-zV2ZRob1gKgeDPJH5wlEGwbcmvYMnbDSLMvJgB1HW_mSOXQj3Q79o9q6oZTipSziwhD68Wf1eZtxlb4nOI-2YNJa4-RtBOg1Ewq_EHae0w6ng3SjM71oLWv9eWNmWumM9wLACln4RdgofaOPXdJps4QBqU3uO-P2hA84TjU_5yfSrkYswxnzgaT5TFDslRcIlut8FqS4erITrKTS3Yb2FhQrnX2JADAQLTdvSxtnJeT8heGmoKBvLXYjLplPtk10aHraCJq9Us41PFfQLaRMc3iAk3apTZ6gBWYc3gTcl-Kz74nnokMZy7FQSCMTVC95TBK8gu37LxG4UVRkZiBnd-5HflvTKtIPOuA9I7GIeClg%3D%3D "Will you please chip in before midnight?")_**

Friend, I believe our country is hungry for positive, conservative leadership.

It is time for us to restore hope, create opportunities, and protect America. **It is time for us to be proud to be Americans and proud of our president.**

**_[That's why I announced I am running for President of the United States.](http://links.ourdefenseofamerica.com/u/click?_t=aa3ef0db03454a26b650c9ca7e919386&_m=1dfb68f43e594636b05eb372ef2b883d&_e=2dWzBPbGvtccb912kmteSnIoCV_RkPzq_ewbRKEBu-zV2ZRob1gKgeDPJH5wlEGwmVm96P7umRxJQg4NDbWyJDNRIhW3MwBQNQ0LoAgY0bvfn9L_gHmBzRhCjNHHDAxRsjtr6c7bfxjbnml42i88jVCsTCYRsuuPLRUHvfGgBxx35ZYSjbeep7iiv1x0Cp-j1jyAggfC6pjA1IaevPTA5nzXAiUIjItm8OzRyvaK9Dr7sG32mfTLShD972nuRwJ7j_QC-lXcXHIbV59YScQXKGpIZi3wX3YyL2B7RaLUs1BWx0MHU_MusO6PC_O3qx3JZslzGZbPd6_yg0YYrsgyiSqSEmTqVLoGTeKKJgeOdFrKhYLd9dVkaYgYZTQO-ZP8owC4XlCeYvZFjVDMZKiG2g%3D%3D "That’s why I announced I am running for President of the United States.")_**

| | [SUPPORT MY CAMPAIGN HERE](http://links.ourdefenseofamerica.com/u/click?_t=aa3ef0db03454a26b650c9ca7e919386&_m=1dfb68f43e594636b05eb372ef2b883d&_e=2dWzBPbGvtccb912kmteSnIoCV_RkPzq_ewbRKEBu-zV2ZRob1gKgeDPJH5wlEGwmVm96P7umRxJQg4NDbWyJDNRIhW3MwBQNQ0LoAgY0bvfn9L_gHmBzRhCjNHHDAxRsjtr6c7bfxjbnml42i88jVCsTCYRsuuPLRUHvfGgBxx35ZYSjbeep7iiv1x0Cp-j1jyAggfC6pjA1IaevPTA5nzXAiUIjItm8OzRyvaK9Dr7sG32mfTLShD972nuRwJ7vyH2OZQ6bMGpBJ3VkJTKU6qOYhZvBRUDRQ8YIN_-WARw7Q3xKm9xtWca3Qy-KoUdJR6sssDeJ3qUpqWVvAlT2UQXq2k7ka5Q6ZHWb2v80ie-G96g2j56kSmFzpzO8bjN2WAOTHkMNQva776Tbk4_FA%3D%3D "SUPPORT MY CAMPAIGN HERE")

The Democrats have chosen a culture of grievance over greatness. Here's the thing though - I know the power of the American Dream _because I have lived it._ My family went from cotton to congress in a **single lifetime.**

**This isn 't going to be easy.** I'll need your help to win.

---

**_[Can you chip in $5, $10 or even $15 to help me win?](http://links.ourdefenseofamerica.com/u/click?_t=aa3ef0db03454a26b650c9ca7e919386&_m=1dfb68f43e594636b05eb372ef2b883d&_e=2dWzBPbGvtccb912kmteSnIoCV_RkPzq_ewbRKEBu-zV2ZRob1gKgeDPJH5wlEGwmVm96P7umRxJQg4NDbWyJDNRIhW3MwBQNQ0LoAgY0bvfn9L_gHmBzRhCjNHHDAxRsjtr6c7bfxjbnml42i88jVCsTCYRsuuPLRUHvfGgBxx35ZYSjbeep7iiv1x0Cp-j1jyAggfC6pjA1IaevPTA5nzXAiUIjItm8OzRyvaK9Dr7sG32mfTLShD972nuRwJ7RhG3k-EE9Te5vXi21V3fdMIO-hBcuUdm_4_e_dz24Ta9umdTC5hMD_NFXaVVf_XVOny4MVNcoLGcl8cGu0-WbqEaF-b1TSKxM-wP_aRb8rAFwjfeeEHevF2VBA-nKb8dzqK9RNRuKZiTuxLKF5PTrA%3D%3D "Can you chip in $5, $10 or even $15 to help me win?")_**

This campaign relies on very generous gifts from people just like you. Trust me, I know that times are tough in Joe Biden's America, so even if it's just $5 I would be so grateful.

Thank you for your support!

-Tim

"Will you please chip in before midnight?" | | | "CHIP IN BEFORE MIDNIGHT" God Bless. Tim | | | "TIM SCOTT IS RUNNING FOR PRESIDENT OF THE UNITED STATES - TELL TIM HE'S GOT YOUR SUPPORT!" Friend, I believe our country is hungry for positive, conservative leadership. It is time for us to restore hope, create opportunities, and protect America. It is time for us to be proud to be Americans and proud of our president. "That’s why I announced I am running for President of the United States." | | "SUPPORT MY CAMPAIGN HERE" The Democrats have chosen a culture of grievance over greatness. Here's the thing though - I know the power of the American Dream because I have lived it. My family went from cotton to congress in a single lifetime. This isn 't going to be easy. I'll need your help to win. | "I NEED YOUR HELP TO WIN!" "Can you chip in $5, $10 or even $15 to help me win?"** This campaign relies on very generous gifts from people just like you. Trust me, I know that times are tough in Joe Biden's America, so even if it's just $5 I would be so grateful. Thank you for your support! -Tim | PAID FOR BY TIM SCOTT FOR AMERICA 1405 ASHLEY RIVER ROAD CHARLESTON, SC 29407

clean_body: I'm leading the charge to banish him... | Friend, Adam Schiff is unfit to hold elected office. He abused his position on the House Intelligence committee to knowingly push the Trump-Russia collusion LIE that tore our country apart. His dishonorable conduct cost taxpayers MILLIONS of dollars, authorized spying on a U.S. President, and he just doubled down on his LIE after the Durham report was released. Adam Schiff is a disgrace, and he needs to be held accountable. These aren't just empty words, Friend. I 'm doing something about it. And I'm not talking about investigating him, censoring him, condemning him, or some other slap on the wrist. I 'm leading the charge to permanently EXPEL Adam Schiff from the House of Representatives so we can permanently banish him from Congress. The Durham report finally revealed the TRUTH about the Trump-Russia collusion hoax for the entire world to see. The report outlined that there was NO CREDIBLE EVIDENCE whatsoever to support the investigation, and it never should have been launched. Even liberal CNN has admitted that Special Counsel John Durham's report was "devastating to the FBI" and acknowledged "it does exonerate Donald Trump". But simply revealing the truth isn 't enough. We've got to hold those responsible for pushing this lie accountable - or they'll be all but certain to do it again. That starts with EXPELLING Adam Schiff from Congress. He was the ringleader of pushing the now-debunked Russia collusion hoax. Along with Deep State Democrats and his far-Left colleagues in the House, he actively worked to undermine the will of the American people to sabotage a duly elected President. For more than 4 years, President Trump faced relentless attacks, witch hunts, and false narratives based on the LIE that Schiff peddled. Adam Schiff is a stain on the House of Representatives, Friend. He shouldn't spend another day in Congress. Team, servant leadership is important to me. As a former Army Ranger, servant leadership meant putting the needs of the lowest-ranking Private and every other team member ahead of my own. That meant leading by example and being the first to jump out of the plane. _Every single time._ I’ve always been driven to be part of something bigger than myself. That’s continued even after serving in the Army. When I saw that Colorado’s values were under assault in Washington, I felt that call to serve again. Since then, I’ve channeled that servant leadership to fight so no Coloradan is left behind. It’s not easy, but it must be done. Our community deserves nothing less. Truth be told, this will be a tough election. Donald Trump, Kevin McCarthy, and GOP leaders are ready to throw everything to flip Colorado red. But I won’t let the odds stop me from fighting. **[To win, I’m going to need your help. That’s why I’m personally asking: Will you pitch in $5 or more to help me continue fighting in Congress?
